data.title: Philippine court upholds murder verdict in drug war case: what does it mean for Duterte?
data.description:
The Philippine Supreme Court has upheld the murder conviction of three policemen who killed a teenage boy at the height of former president Rodrigo Duterte’s war on drugs in 2017, a watershed moment that legal analysts say affirms the country’s justice system.
In a decision made public on Monday, the court found Arnel Oares, Jeremias Pereda and Jerwin Cruz guilty of murdering 17-year-old high school student Kian delos Santos while conducting an anti-drug operation in the city of Caloocan in Metro Manila.
They were each sentenced to 40 years’ jail and would have to pay the teen’s family a total of 275,000 pesos (US$4,700).
The decision affirmed earlier rulings by a regional trial court and the Court of Appeals that had convicted the policemen without eligibility for parole, despite multiple appeals from the defendants.
The policemen were first found guilty in 2018, a year after delos Santos’ death.

Opposition senator Risa Hontiveros, who led Senate investigations into delos Santos’ murder in 2017, lauded the ruling.
“The Supreme Court’s decision is clear: Kian delos Santos had been helplessly killed. There was no encounter, and he did not fight back. The three officers’ alibis have no truth to them,” she said on Tuesday.
The decision was “an inspiring development for human rights advocates, lawyers and civic groups, and other families of tokhang victims,” she said, using the colloquial term for the drug war.
Delos Santos’ case proved that the killings during the drug war were systemic, she said. “Accountability should also be demanded from officials who attempted to cover up the incident, and even made up lies about Kian and his family to justify his death.”
This is the first of a growing list of cases involving minors killed during Duterte’s war on drugs. Although police have admitted to killing over 6,000 throughout the drug war from 2016 to 2022, human rights watchdogs estimate this number to be much higher, at about 30,000.
Shot while kneeling
Analysts say the court’s decision was significant as it affirmed the correct application of self-defence, which the police had used to justify their killing of delos Santos.
Earlier police reports stated that the teen had attempted to flee when the officers approached him, while also drawing a firearm, prompting Oares to fire back in self-defence. Delos Santos was later found with a pistol, cartridges and two sachets of methamphetamine.
However, surveillance footage and eyewitness accounts refuted the claims. Videos showed that after having beaten delos Santos and accused him of possessing illegal drugs, the police dragged him into a dark alleyway, where he was shot.
An autopsy showed delos Santos was likely kneeling on the ground when he was shot, consistent with the regional court’s findings in 2018. The Court of Appeals also affirmed this ruling.
This was in line with witnesses saying they heard delos Santos pleading for his life, saying he had an exam at school the next day.

The sequence of events established treachery, an important element in ascertaining murder. According to the Supreme Court, treachery “exists when the victim has no chance to defend themselves, and the attacker deliberately chooses the means or method of attack”.
Ross Tugade, an international legal scholar and assistant counsel to the International Criminal Court (ICC), told This Week in Asia that the Philippine court’s decision was significant as delos Santos’ case “has gripped our collective sense of the pursuit of justice in the drug war”.
“Kian’s case is what we could call in international criminal investigations as ‘emblematic cases’ or those that demonstrate the pattern and methods of mass atrocity crimes,” he said.
“As Kian’s killing took place during the peak of the drug war, the circumstances surrounding it revealed the workings of the state policy during Duterte’s time – namely arbitrary killing, ‘one time big time’ operations with quotas, false narratives on innocent people, and the planting of evidence, among others.”
The court further dismissed Oares’ argument that he shot delos Santos while performing his duties as a police officer, adding that “this defence applies only when the harm is an unavoidable result of a lawful police action”.
“The killing of a minor could not be considered standard in this operation”, it said, adding that the “performance of duties does not include murder”.
‘Very encouraging’
Despite the death arising from a state-sanctioned operation, the ruling was consistent with criminal law and the Supreme Court’s adherence to its principles, Darwin Angeles, a lecturer at the University of the Philippines’ College of Law, said, calling it “very encouraging for similarly situated victims”.
The court also rejected officers’ defence that the teen’s death had been a regular consequence of police operations. In the past, the former president had admitted that he had encouraged officers to provoke suspects into fighting back so they could be killed.
“Duterte encouraged the use by police officers of lethal force on the premise that they can exculpate themselves later on with a claim of self-defence. This case shows that Duterte, despite being a former prosecutor, was mistaken,” Angeles said.
However, analysts said that the Supreme Court’s decision, although significant, might only marginally affect Duterte’s ICC case.


The former president remains detained at The Hague and awaits trial for charges of crimes against humanity for the killings committed during his administration’s bloody drug war.
“The [court’s] ruling in Kian’s case can help the prosecution’s case albeit marginally,” Angeles said. So while evidence of wrongdoing by police officers can be helpful, the defence can argue that delos Santos’ case was isolated.
However, the decision may further prove the necessity of the ICC in exercising its jurisdiction over Duterte’s case.
One conviction over “thousands of questionable killings” and the length of time needed to “fully secure accountability” showed that the country’s judicial system might not be adequate, Angeles said.
Tugade said that the Supreme Court’s decision had affirmed one case of murder, which could not be contextualised within a larger pattern of crimes against humanity. In addition, those convicted were “foot soldiers”, not high-profile officials who had shaped the war on drugs.
“This will likely not trigger issues of admissibility because the ICC is interested in the pattern of crimes against humanity and the perpetrator structure, especially those most responsible for the crime,” Tugade said.

data.title: China’s offshore yuan breaks major benchmark as appreciation momentum builds
data.description:
China's offshore yuan strengthened further on Thursday, dipping below the benchmark rate of seven against the US dollar – another sign of the currency's continued appreciation after a brief breach of the same threshold on Wednesday evening.
The recent fluctuations mark the currency’s first appreciation past the major psychological marker in 15 months, suggesting a change in market sentiment and providing more support for the global investors and economists who have argued the currency has been undervalued.
The offshore yuan’s exchange rate reached a high of 6.9960 on Thursday morning, according to figures from Chinese financial data provider Wind, after briefly moving to 6.9999 on Wednesday. The onshore yuan, meanwhile, hit 7.01 against the US dollar on Thursday. Both rates were reached for the first time since September 2024.
Market optimism for the Chinese currency continues to rise, with analysts predicting the yuan will appreciate further, though small and medium-sized exporters may have concerns over the pressures a stronger yuan could exert on their operations.
The yuan’s recent strengthening reflects both a weaker US dollar and shifts in the supply and demand of foreign exchange, analysts said.
Sustained trade surpluses and concentrated settlement of foreign exchange among companies have provided a temporary boost in demand for China's currency, compounded by investor concerns related to the sustainability of US government debt.
On Thursday, the People’s Bank of China set its daily reference rate, also known as the midpoint or fixing rate, at 7.0392 per US dollar. The onshore yuan has appreciated by more than 1 per cent against the US currency over the past month.
At a meeting last week, the bank's monetary policy committee emphasised the need to minimise the risk of exchange rate overshooting and maintain “basic stability at a reasonable and balanced level”, according to a statement released on Wednesday.
Over the past few months, the bank's language on exchange rates has alternated between “resilience” and “flexibility,” Goldman Sachs analysts wrote in a note on Thursday.
This suggests the PBOC “may have developed a preference for a stronger yuan while remaining keen to avoid too rapid an appreciation", they said.
China has surpassed a US$1 trillion trade surplus, and we all know the yuan’s appreciation is unstoppable
Tan Jian, Guangdong
Zhao Wei, chief economist at Shenwan Hongyuan Securities, said in a Tuesday report that the demand for pending foreign exchange settlement has been high this year, with non-finance companies’ foreign exchange deposits surpassing the record US$561.8 billion seen in February 2022.
However, some of China’s export-focused manufacturers have said the sustained yuan appreciation is putting a strain on their activity.
“China has surpassed a US$1 trillion trade surplus, and we all know the yuan’s appreciation is unstoppable,” said Tan Jian, owner of a lighting supply company in Guangdong province, in a recent video posted on social media.
“But this increases risks for our small and medium-sized exporting enterprises in raw material procurement, order pricing and payment terms.”
The weakening US dollar has also prompted China’s individual investors to shift from the American currency into other assets to diversify their holdings.
“I exchanged US$50,000 annually over the past two years, but I’ve stopped this year,” said Guangzhou-based translator Yuki Su. “Recently, I’ve been steadily increasing my investments in gold and silver funds.”
Several of the country’s economists and policymakers have posited that the currency is entering a phase of long-term appreciation.
A report by Yingda Securities on Wednesday projected a steady period of appreciation in 2026 and potentially 2027, eventually reaching 6.20 to 6.30 against the US dollar. They based their estimate on China’s solid economic fundamentals, narrowing interest rate differentials between the two countries and pressure on the US dollar index.
Huang Qifan, the outspoken former mayor of Chongqing who now serves as a senior government adviser, said in Shenzhen on Sunday that the yuan will gradually appreciate to around 6.0 against the dollar over the next decade.
Additional reporting by Carol Yang
[1]
[2]

data.title: Chill to deepen on Boxing Day after Hongkongers wake up to cold Christmas
data.description:
This story has been made freely available as a public service to our readers. Please consider supporting SCMP’s journalism by subscribing. Get faster notifications on the latest updates by downloading our app.
Hongkongers woke up to a cold Christmas Day, but the weather is expected to get even colder on Boxing Day, with temperatures set to drop further to 13 degrees Celsius (55.4 Fahrenheit).
The Hong Kong Observatory said on Thursday that the city was “appreciably cooler” under the influence of a northeast monsoon, adding that temperatures ranged from 13 to 17 degrees.
Temperatures were generally six to eight degrees lower than at noon on Wednesday, the weather forecaster said, noting that they were expected to drop further at night.
“Temperatures on [Friday] morning will be around 13 degrees in urban areas and it will be generally cold across the New Territories,” it said.
Moderate to fresh northerly winds are also expected, with strong winds offshore and over high ground.
The Observatory advised residents to keep warm, and take care of the elderly and those with chronic medical conditions.
The mercury at Tai Mo Shan, Hong Kong’s highest peak at 957 metres, dropped to 5.6 degrees at around 11am.
The dry monsoon will continue to affect Guangdong from the weekend into early next week, bringing fine weather, according to the forecaster.
“The temperature difference between day and night will be rather large over inland areas,” it said.

data.title: 7 stories from 2025 that made us feel good, including Jimmy O. Yang and Cantopop DJs
data.description:
The festive season is all about spreading cheer, so here we take a look back on some of our favourite feel-good stories of 2025.
Hong Kong artist Joannah Hon, who paints local people and then reveals the art in public, made us happy with her work, and Aiden Tsang’s wholesome Instagram reels, where the eight-year-old teaches Cantonese, also put us in a good mood.
American photographer Austin Bell also put a smile on our faces thanks to his project that saw him photograph, from above, all of Hong Kong’s outdoor basketball courts, turning the images into geometric art.
Here, we revisit seven fun stories of 2025.
1. This Hong Kong artist’s art has gone IG viral
In her short videos, which are often paired with classic Cantonese songs, Joannah Hon Pui-yan reveals her oversized oil paintings of local cultural icons on the streets of Hong Kong.
The 32-year-old artist – known as Jobey – poses next to the subjects that her paintings depict, including taxi drivers, fishmongers and even women doing tai chi.
She began posting content on her Instagram account @jobeyhon in June, and her most viral video is one in which she reveals a painting in the middle of a wet market in Hong Kong.
Read the full story here
2. A famed Chinese restaurant in Paris that drew the stars

For almost 40 years, Davé, a Chinese restaurant in the heart of Paris, attracted the who’s who of fashion, film, art and music.
Opened in 1982, it was not just a place to eat, but an exclusive, intimate salon where celebrities, artists and designers gathered for what felt like a private, timeless film sequence.
Conversation and creative juices flowed as fast as the champagne, and at the centre of it all was the owner, Hong Kong-born Tai “Davé” Cheung, a charismatic host who spent nights weaving his way through the dimly lit tables, often with his beloved cat Momo in tow, chatting with a cast of celebrities.
Read the full story here
3. Want to learn Cantonese? This 8-year-old will teach you

There is something about Aiden Tsang’s Instagram reels that instantly puts you in a good mood.
The eight-year-old’s videos – a combination of innocence and cuteness, delivered with a huge cheeky smile – are the perfect antidote to much of the inane content flooding social media.
Born in Hong Kong, Aiden, who moved with his parents to the Western Australian capital of Perth, admits he is a little homesick. But he has found a creative way to stay connected to his home, thanks to his Instagram account @cantohkaiden, where he shares lessons on how to speak Cantonese.
Read the full story here
4. For Jimmy O. Yang, Hong Kong feels like home

Back in 2014, between the first and second seasons of the HBO comedy series Silicon Valley, in which he had a supporting role, Jimmy O. Yang spent his days moonlighting as an Uber driver.
The Hong Kong-born Hollywood hopeful used a pay cheque for his role in the series as Jian-Yang – he received the Screen Actors Guild minimum of US$900 a day – to buy a used Toyota Prius hybrid, which he would drive around Los Angeles to earn the money to pay his rent.
Read the full story here
5. These DJs are rekindling Hong Kong’s love of Cantopop

The three members of Beat Friday do not look like your typical DJs when they take to the decks.
Dressed in suits and ties, or even braces and gold-rimmed glasses, the group have curated their image to remind party-goers of the golden age of Cantopop.
From the 1980s to the 2000s, the music genre was one of the most unifying cultural forces in Asia, and Hong Kong was an entertainment powerhouse that dominated cinema and music in the region.
Read the full story here
6. Hong Kong’s 2,549 outdoor basketball courts become art

In 2017, American photographer Austin Bell visited Choi Hung Estate, a public housing estate in Hong Kong’s Kowloon district that is also an Instagram hotspot popular with residents and tourists, who flock there to photograph its colourful facade.
It was his first visit to the city and, like many, he was captivated by the vibrant hues of the estate’s buildings, which were named Choi Hung after the Cantonese for “rainbow”.
But what really struck a chord were the bold patterns covering the estate’s basketball courts.
Read the full story here
7. Meet the Chinese dancer who loves Indian dance

Silver jewellery, the flick of an eye and intricate hand gestures that flow to the melody – Chau Hoi-shan, known by her stage name Shanti, skilfully weaves sounds and movements into her Bollywood dance routine.
Chau, who was born in China and moved to Hong Kong when she was 15, was always a dancer – but her interest was in a genre that you might not expect.
“When I was a child, I was exposed to Chinese dance,” she recalls. “However, I watched Indian movies as a child, and the dance scenes in them were very captivating and brought me joy.”
Read the full story here

data.title: Australia investigates after car with Hanukkah sign torched in Christmas Day attack
data.description:
Australian police are investigating a “suspicious fire” after a car carrying a sign celebrating the Jewish festival of Hanukkah was set ablaze on Thursday in Melbourne.
The empty car, which had a “Happy Chanukah” sign fixed to the roof, was scorched by the fire while parked in a house driveway, images on national broadcaster ABC television showed.
It was set alight in the early hours of Thursday morning in the Melbourne suburb of St Kilda East, Victoria police said in a statement, describing it as a “suspicious fire”.
Occupants of the house were evacuated as a precaution.
“Detectives have identified a person who may be able to assist with their investigation and they are actively searching for and making inquiries into their whereabouts,” police said.


Australian authorities are stiffening laws and penalties for hate crimes after a December 14 mass shooting at a Hanukkah festival on Bondi Beach killed 15 people.
Rabbi Effy Block, of the local Chabad of St Kilda, said it was clearly an antisemitic attack.
“Thank God no people were harmed,” he said.
“But this is a continuing escalation, where we see these events happening again and again,” he added.
“My Jewish community in St Kilda and Melbourne do not feel safe in their own homes and country.”

data.title: Year in golf: Rory McIlroy finally joins elite club that Scottie Scheffler could join soon
data.description:
Rory McIlroy’s long-awaited Masters victory in April made him the first golfer in 25 years to join the sport’s most exclusive club, but Scottie Scheffler’s two-major haul in 2025 raised the possibility that the next career grand slam winner could be just around the corner.
McIlroy, in his 11th attempt to complete the career grand slam, had to endure a topsy-turvy final round at Augusta National where he squandered a four-shot lead but then emerged from a crowded pack to prevail in a sudden-death playoff.
After draining a four-foot birdie putt for the win, the Northern Irishman raised his arms before releasing years of pent-up frustration as he dropped to his knees and began to sob, providing one of the lasting images of the year in golf.
“There wasn’t much joy in that reaction. It was all relief,” McIlroy said after the win. “And then, you know, the joy came pretty soon after that. But that was … a decade-plus of emotion that came out of me there.”
By winning the Masters, McIlroy joined Gene Sarazen, Ben Hogan, Gary Player, Jack Nicklaus and Tiger Woods as the only men to complete the career grand slam of golf’s four majors.
That elite group could soon have a new member.

Sensational Scheffler
World No 1 Scheffler showed why he remains a cut above his peers in 2025 after leading the PGA Tour in wins with six, including convincing major triumphs at the PGA Championship and British Open. He was fourth at the Masters and seventh at the US Open.
Scheffler, who won the Masters in 2022 and 2024, now has three legs of the career grand slam and will get his first crack at completing his set of major titles in June at Shinnecock Hills where the final round of the US Open will be held on the American’s 30th birthday.
The dominance displayed by Scheffler, who won the PGA Tour Player of the Year Award for a fourth consecutive year, has drawn comparisons to 15-time major winner Woods. Barring injury, Scheffler will be the betting favourite at every major next year.
“I don’t think we thought the golfing world would see someone as dominant as Tiger come through so soon, and here’s Scottie sort of taking that throne of dominance,” two-time major champion Xander Schauffele said at the British Open.
“You can’t even say he’s on a run. He’s just been killing it for over two years now.”

Life-changing putt
American J.J. Spaun enjoyed a remarkable resurgence in 2025 as he went from journeyman status to major champion thanks to an improbable walk-off birdie putt from 64 feet (19.5 metres) to win the US Open by two shots in challenging conditions at treacherous Oakmont.
A late-afternoon storm saturated Oakmont during the final round and caused a 96-minute weather delay, but a fearless Spaun, who looked well out of contention after five early bogeys, wielded his trusty putter like a magic wand down the closing stretch for a life-changing win.
“It’s definitely like a storybook, fairy tale ending, kind of underdog fighting back, not giving up, never quitting,” Spaun said. “With the rain and everything and then the putt, I mean, you couldn’t write a better story. I’m just so fortunate to be on the receiving end of that.”
Bedlam at Bethpage Black
New York’s Bethpage Black was the site of the game’s most prestigious team event but this year golf became a sideshow to an intense atmosphere at a Ryder Cup played amid torrents of verbal abuse and inappropriate behaviour from the home crowd.
McIlroy, a fan favourite almost every week on the PGA Tour, was the primary target of the abuse, and after Europe won the Ryder Cup on foreign soil for the first time in 13 years he said, “golf should be held to a higher standard than this”.

On the course, Europe narrowly avoided the biggest collapse in Ryder Cup history by holding off a Sunday charge from the United States.
Tyrrell Hatton, in the penultimate match, secured the outright victory when he earned a half-point from his battle with Collin Morikawa that put Europe at the 14½ points needed to win the Ryder Cup.
Civil war
Early in the year there had been optimism that the PGA Tour and Saudi-funded LIV Golf were close to finalising the framework agreement that was announced in June 2023, but a deal to end golf’s civil war no longer seems imminent.
Earlier this month, LIV Golf CEO Scott O’Neil said he was in regular contact with PGA Tour CEO Brian Rolapp but they were not in “serious negotiation” at that point.

data.title: Let 2026 be the year we are kinder to animals
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
The New Year is a time for renewal, not just for us, but for the animals who share our city and our planet. As Hong Kong prepares to welcome 2026, we face an important question: will we continue to accept animal suffering as entertainment and “tradition”, or will we choose compassion and progress?
Over the past year, there have been clear signs that change is possible. Across Asia, public pressure has exposed abuse and pushed industries to evolve – from investigations revealing violence against angora rabbits and elephants forced into performances, to broader acceptance of modern, non-animal testing methods and the rapid expansion of vegan dining. These shifts point to a simple truth: when people demand better for animals, industries respond.
Hong Kong has a role to play in this movement. Take Ocean Park, a venue that promotes itself as a conservation leader while continuing to profit from captive animal displays and from serving animals in its restaurants. Peta has repeatedly urged Ocean Park to align its practices with its stated values by ending dolphin shows and removing fish and shellfish from its menus. These steps would be consistent. Conservation loses its meaning when it excludes the animals most directly affected.
Hong Kong will welcome the Year of the Horse in February next year. Horses symbolise freedom, strength and spirit in Chinese culture. Yet in Hong Kong, real horses are forced to race at extreme speeds, whipped, drugged and pushed until their legs shatter. Honouring the Year of the Horse should prompt us to question why industries that treat living beings as expendable are defended as entertainment or “tradition”.
Animals are not objects or resources. They experience fear, stress and pleasure, form relationships and value their own lives. Recognising this shapes healthier communities and a more sustainable future. Vegan food systems reduce environmental harm, animal-free science drives innovation without victims, and ending animal-based entertainment reflects values rooted in respect rather than control.
So what can people in Hong Kong do in the new year?
Start with your plate. Going vegan spares animals immense harm and reduces environmental strain. Support restaurants and businesses that embrace animal-free choices.
Say no to animal entertainment. Refuse to buy tickets to dolphin shows, horse races or any attraction that profits from confinement and coercion. Let venues like Ocean Park know that Hong Kong expects better.
Choose cruelty-free fashion and products. Avoid fur, exotic skins and cosmetics tested on animals, and support brands that commit to ethical practices.
And finally, speak up. Progress happens when individuals raise their voices – by asking businesses to change, sharing information and supporting efforts that challenge outdated, harmful practices.
Hong Kong’s culture has always evolved – shaped by conscience, creativity and courage. In 2026, choosing kindness towards animals can be part of that story, reflecting a city that values empathy as much as tradition and progress as much as prosperity.
Jason Baker, president, People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals (Peta) Asia
In age of instant gratification, contentment is a lost art
“Convenient”, “quick” and “simple” – we see these words everywhere, used in advertisements and promotions to convince us to make a purchase. While looking to make one’s life easier is what drives us to advance, in the scramble for the quick hit of serotonin or the 3 per cent increase in sales, we have become intolerant of discomfort.
In our pursuit of results, we have also forgotten that the process is important. Some people are so hasty to create that they forfeit the process of creation itself and ask generative AI to do it for them. We shun struggling with the process and fixate on the results. We’ve been so obsessed with “levelling up” in life that we have forgotten to stop and smell the roses.
At the same time, we have become blind to satisfaction. Your phone camera is already capturing at megapixels so high the eye cannot see the difference; your running shoes are so optimal it only matters to athletes. Marketing pushes us to always catch up with the next big thing, sending the message that anything less than the best is not good enough. This hunger for perfection is starving us of the contentment that comes from the accomplishments we’ve made so far and the happiness we already have.
All this came to me while I was patiently waiting for my game console to boot up. It didn’t matter that it could load games at 10 times the speed and had a higher resolution; I was just satisfied that I got some cool games to play.
Joy Fan, Sha Tin

data.title: Singapore’s viral moments of 2025: politician slip-ups, sinkhole heroes and a new US envoy
data.description:
From political gaffes and diplomatic stumbles to courtroom fury and security breaches, many of Singapore’s viral moments in 2025 showed how quickly everyday incidents in the city state could turn into national focal points. Passionate online discussions have revealed deep concerns about power, accountability and how the country sees itself – and is seen by others.

Locker-room rage
Singapore’s acting sports minister came under fire after he was caught calling the Hong Kong men’s football team and their supporters “idiots” on a live stream in November.
He was speaking to Singapore’s team in the locker room after they beat Hong Kong 2-1 in an Asian Cup qualifier at Kai Tak Stadium.
“It was fantastic … you kena [Malay for hit] pressured by them, all the fans were bloody idiots, end up players also played like idiots [sic] … But you all played like lions,” Neo said.
His comments drew backlash on social media, with users saying his words showed poor sportsmanship.
Neo later retracted his comments and conceded that he “probably should have been more respectful”.
According to a spokeswoman from Hong Kong’s Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau, Neo made a private apology to Rosanna Law, his Hong Kong counterpart, following the saga.

Diplomatic doubts
Anjani Sinha, then US ambassador-nominee to Singapore, failed to impress residents in the city state after he could not answer questions posed by Senator Tammy Duckworth in July.
Sinha was unable to pinpoint the United States’ trade surplus with Singapore and when the city state would next chair regional bloc Asean, leading to comments from Duckworth that he needed to “shape up and do some homework”.
Duckworth described Singapore as one of America’s most important friends in the Indo-Pacific and a key place for “fighting against” China.
Social media users doubled down on Duckworth’s criticism, with one saying Sinha had been nominated for the position simply because he played golf with US President Donald Trump. Sinha was officially confirmed as ambassador in October.
Sinha drew ire again when he defended Trump’s tariffs on Singapore, framing the levy as the US seeking help from a friend after “playing an important role” in the city state’s economic miracle.
Analysts and social media users said bilateral ties were mutually beneficial.

Missed gesture
A politician was accused of snubbing a handshake from an elderly man after a video of their interaction made its rounds online in June, a month after the general election.
Minister of State Gan Siow Huang appeared to shun lion dance troupe member Leong Chee Leng, who was the first to extend his hands as she exited the car. She looked around, placing her phone in her pocket, and shook the hand of another man before giving Leong a weak handshake.
She soon received backlash online, with some accusing her of being dismissive towards commoners. One user said: “If an MP treats a common person with such disdain, how can people trust that they really have the people’s best interests at heart?”
Gan apologised for the incident after it escalated online, and told local media that she was overwhelmed by the group that had greeted her and was trying to identify the host of the event to acknowledge him first.

Red carpet breach
A serial intruder was slammed for giving Singapore a bad name after he jumped American popstar Ariana Grande at the film premiere of Wicked: For Good in November.
Australian Johnson Wen, 26, was later jailed for nine days, a punishment that many lauded for setting a precedent, yet some felt was insufficient.
Online users said Wen’s actions tarnished Singapore’s reputation as a safe haven, and called for caning to be added to his punishment.
“I’m glad to hear he is in prison. He should be in prison for longer because he has done the same thing to other celebrities,” one user said.
Wen is known for disrupting high-profile events and sharing his misbehaviour on social media. In court in Singapore in November, Wen said he would not repeat such actions. He was bounced out of Lady Gaga’s concert earlier this month.

Underground heroes
Migrant workers rushed to save a woman after her car was swallowed by a 3-metre deep sinkhole in eastern Singapore in July, drawing praise from social media users as videos of their heroics gained traction.
A handful of workers from a nearby construction site threw a rope to the driver and pulled her to safety, rescuing her within five minutes. The car sank into murky water soon after.
Social media users thanked the workers for their quick response, lauding them as “heroes” and calling for greater recognition for their actions.
“This isn’t the first time foreign workers have stepped up as true heroes – risking their own lives to save others,” a user said.
The incident also reignited debate on the treatment of migrant workers in Singapore, including the contentious practice of transporting labourers in the open cargo decks of lorries.
In a comment on a video interview with Pitchai Udaiyappan Subbiah, one of the men who saved the driver, a user said: “It’s time we reward them with proper transport and not just [in the] back of the lorry. They save our lives while we endanger theirs. How can we do that?”

Localising US propaganda?
Social media users also criticised America for bringing its rivalry with China to Singapore, after a US embassy video likened corridor clutter in the city state’s public housing blocks to Beijing’s territorial and assertive actions in the disputed South China Sea.
Some online users said the video – posted in May to the embassy’s social media pages – was an attempt to localise US propaganda, while others who resonated with the clip’s message warned against China’s aggression.
Without directly referencing the video, Singapore’s foreign ministry urged resolution through “appropriate channels” and said it rejected “attempts by foreign embassies to incite domestic reactions to international issues involving third countries”.

Looks debate
A viral image on social media depicting colour sketches of men from different regions in China stirred debate on the physical appearance of Singaporean Chinese based on their ancestry, with some online users concluding they were the least attractive of the gene pool.
But psychotherapists were quick to dismiss the discussion, warning that it spurred the harmful perpetuation of racism and warped beauty standards.
In the depictions, the male face from southeastern China is shown to have a rounder nose, protruding jaw and a face less angular than his counterparts. More than two-thirds of Chinese Singaporeans belong to dialect groups who have roots in the region.
Some online users claimed the sketches were representative of the unattractiveness of ethnic Chinese in the city state, while others rebuffed that line of argument by saying that beauty was subjective.

Screen offence
US crime drama Countdown drew online criticism after a Singaporean character in the TV series was depicted to be unable to speak English.
Kieran Teo, a Singaporean character, spoke Malay during interrogation by police after a murder occurred on his company premises. People’s Action Party representatives soon interrupted the session, aiming to end it abruptly.
Online users said the writers were ignorant, and criticised the series for inaccurately portraying Singaporeans as being unable to speak English and the ruling PAP as deceitful. Some urged for an apology from showrunners.
A user poked fun at the choice of Teo’s company name and the character speaking Malay with an Indonesian accent. “And Beruang Holdings? At least come up with something more believable,” the user said.
Others noted that misrepresentations of Singapore in Hollywood were common, citing the example of how Crazy Rich Asians insinuated that all Singaporeans were Chinese and lived ultra-wealthy lives.

Limits of remorse
Former actor Ian Fang received little sympathy after bowing in apology for having sex with a 15-year-old girl, with some online users calling for a harsher sentence.
The China-born actor, 36, was sentenced to 40 months’ jail in May after pleading guilty to sexual offences with a minor.
“When you make a mistake, you have to take responsibility. Of course, I also hope that everyone can give me a chance to be reborn, to start over,” Fang said in June, when he appeared at the State Courts to begin his sentence.
Social media users seemed sceptical of his apology, with some urging a longer term or caning as punishment.
“He should be given 20 strokes of rotan [cane] too,” one user said.
Court records revealed that Fang and the teenage girl had unprotected sex on multiple occasions after meeting at an entertainment event in 2024. Fang also admitted harassing the girl during the investigation, including pressuring her to plead for leniency on his behalf.
The victim’s father similarly urged for Fang to not be spared caning, telling national broadcaster CNA: “Mark him for life. He marked my daughter for life.”

data.title: Southern California drenched by powerful Christmas storms, flash flooding
data.description:
Flash flood warnings were in effect in Los Angeles and most of southern California on Wednesday as one of the worst Christmas storms in recent memory brought heavy rain and fears of deadly mudslides.
Driven by an atmospheric river known as “the Pineapple Express”, which moves heavy moisture from the tropical climes of Hawaii to rain on the West Coast, the storm is expected to deliver months’ worth of rain over the next few days.
California Governor Gavin Newsom declared a state of emergency in several counties, including Los Angeles, to facilitate the rapid mobilisation and allocation of resources if needed.
“Life-threatening flash flooding continues over Southern California today and tomorrow; heavy mountain snow and high winds also ongoing,” the National Weather Service (NWS) said, adding that “lives and property are in great danger.”

The first burst of heavy rain hit the region Tuesday night, leaving Los Angeles with fallen trees and some debris in the streets, as well as minor flooding that hampered traffic.
The rainfall intensified early Wednesday, leaving thousands of people in southern California without power due to downed lines caused by the fury of the Christmas storm.
Late Wednesday the first storm moved out of the Los Angeles area, “but another storm is expected to move in later tonight into Christmas Day,” weather officials warned.
Some communities already received more than 25cm (10 inches) of rain in the first storm, forecasters said.
Across the state, some 80,000 homes and businesses had no electricity heading into Christmas Eve, according to tracking site Poweroutage.us.
Fire burn scars and the communities near them across the state are under special alert, including the coastal areas of Pacific Palisades and Malibu, as they are still recovering from the devastating wildfires in January.

Due to fire damage, the terrain is susceptible to mudslides from heavy rain.
While many were still hoping to drive to spend the holidays with family, transportation officials were busy announcing road and freeway closures due to flooding, fallen trees and mud flows across Southern California.
The Red Cross opened shelters in several communities as some residents in Southern California received immediate evacuation orders.
Authorities were responding to a series of accidents Wednesday, and several major roads were closed due to flash flood warnings.
Ariel Cohen, an NWS meteorologist, warned that from Wednesday afternoon through Friday, “many areas will likely experience significant flooding, along with rockslides and mudslides, especially in the higher elevations”.
“If you were planning to travel on the roads during Christmas, please reconsider your plans,” he added.

However, many Angelenos were still out and about Wednesday, buying last-minute gifts, grocery shopping, even jogging.
For some though, Christmas travel was upended.
“We decided to stay home,” said Jim Lewis, who opted against going to his cousins in nearby Pasadena and was doing last-minute shopping in the city instead.
“We’ve been receiving all these alerts, it doesn’t feel safe, I don’t feel like driving there at dark,” he said.
Larissa Peet, who was planning a party, said her celebration was still on.
“Nothing that we’re doing differently. Just hanging out, eating, drinking and having a good time,” she said.
In addition to the threat of flooding, meteorologists are forecasting wind gusts up to 130km/h (80mph) in the mountains and deserts of Los Angeles County.
Some Californian communities are still reeling from thousands of wildfires that killed 31 people across the state during 2025, including residential neighbourhoods of Los Angeles.
In the Sierra Nevada range along the eastern border of California, more than a 30cm of snow had already fallen this week, with up to 1.5 metres (five feet) forecast before the storm is through.

data.title: 9 held over use of 45 fake surveying equipment certificates in Hong Kong projects
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested nine employees of a company suspected of using surveying equipment with 45 forged certificates in five hospital expansion projects and one construction site.
The force said on Thursday that it had detained six men and three women, aged 23 to 48, from acoustic and environmental services consultancy Novox for allegedly forging at least 45 certificates from four testing centres since 2022.
It added that the nine people, including three company directors and staff members, were detained after receiving reports from several organisations alleging that a subcontractor had used forged documents in government construction projects.
Novox supplied surveying equipment to four Urban Renewal Authority (URA) projects in To Kwa Wan, with the latter confirming that two of them involved devices with forged certificates.
The two projects, located at Bailey Street/Wing Kwong Street and Hung Fook Street/Ngan Hon Street, were awarded to Nation Star Development and Port Destiny, respectively.
Nation Star is owned by Henderson Land Development, while Port Destiny is a subsidiary of Kerry Properties.
Novox also reportedly provided environmental monitoring services for the expansion of Terminal 2 at Hong Kong International Airport.
Earlier this month, the Hospital Authority reported to police that Novox had submitted a suspected forged equipment certificate for the expansion of Princess Margaret Hospital in Kwai Chung, after discovering that the document had not been issued by the mainland Chinese laboratory Guangdong Zhongzhenhang Metrology Testing Co Ltd, as claimed.
Development minister Bernadette Linn Hon-ho earlier defended the construction sector, saying its long-standing reputation for quality and infrastructure development, both regionally and internationally, remained intact.
She acknowledged that some “bad apples” had failed to follow regulations but said the entire industry should not be “tarred with the same brush”.
